rationale_for_baseline_tier: Focal status epilepticus (a >3-hour unremitting seizure not returning to baseline) with concurrent acute aspiration and hypoxia (SpO2 92%, dusky, tachypnoeic) in a non-verbal, fully-dependent, dysphagic adolescent. Both the neurological and airway threats are immediately life-threatening — prolonged status causes irreversible brain injury and the unprotected airway risks fatal obstruction. This is a call-000 emergency (T5); the ambulance provides oxygen, suction, airway backup and hospital-level seizure termination and imaging that telehealth cannot.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Ongoing seizure not stopping / not returning to baseline
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — a seizure beyond his plan's threshold; give his prescribed buccal midazolam per plan while 000 is called
escalation_edges.rationale: Status epilepticus is a time-critical neurological emergency; every minute of continued seizure risks brain injury.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This has gone on far too long to wait — call 000 for an ambulance now. Lay him on his side, and give his prescribed emergency buccal midazolam the way his seizure plan says. Stay on the line with 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Worsening breathing, increasing secretions/regurgitation, or dusky colour (hypoxia)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — stop the feed pump and position on side now
escalation_edges.rationale: Aspiration with hypoxia can progress to airway obstruction and respiratory arrest; needs oxygen and suction.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Turn the feeding pump off straight away and lay him on his side so anything in his mouth drains out. If you are trained and have suction, clear his mouth. Tell 000 his breathing is fast and his colour has changed.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Stops breathing normally, apnoea, or becomes completely unresponsive/collapses
escalation_edges.rationale: Respiratory arrest from airway obstruction or prolonged seizure — dispatcher-guided CPR and paramedic resuscitation are the critical resource.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he stops breathing normally or you cannot rouse him at all, tell 000 immediately and follow their instructions — start CPR if they direct you and you are able.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Non-verbal, fully-dependent patient; all information via a support worker who cannot examine or measure vitals
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for escalation because the patient cannot self-report pain, breathlessness or seizure sensation; treat carer-observed change as reliable and act.
No weight-based dose is calculated or stated. Use only his OWN prescribed emergency medication per his individualised plan; all dosing decisions defer to paramedics/hospital.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Microcephaly, spastic quadriplegia, epilepsy and dysphagia baseline
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Do not attribute the new acute change to his baseline disability (diagnostic overshadowing); a NEW, prolonged, unremitting deterioration in a complex patient is an emergency until proven otherwise.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them it is a prolonged seizure with choking/breathing trouble. Turn OFF the feeding pump. Lay him on his side in the recovery position to protect his airway. If the seizure has lasted longer than his seizure plan allows, give his prescribed emergency buccal midazolam the way the plan describes. Stay with him, keep suction ready if you have it, and unlock the door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours or the next in-hours period.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Have his medication list, seizure management plan and feeding/dysphagia details ready for the paramedics and hospital; the discharge summary should feed back to the GP, paediatric complex-care team, speech pathology and dietetics.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a >3-hour seizure with aspiration and hypoxia as routine/urgent care rather than a 000 emergency, or dismissing it as the patient's baseline disability.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Continued status epilepticus causes irreversible brain injury and the unprotected airway obstructs — leading to hypoxic arrest and death.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising self-transport to ED instead of an ambulance for a patient in active status epilepticus with airway compromise who needs en-route airway management and seizure termination.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Not applicable here — T5 is correct; there is no meaningful over-triage risk in this presentation.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The precise seizure trigger (hypoglycaemia, infection, metabolic derangement) and the degree of aspiration cannot be determined remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Do not attempt to resolve the trigger at home; escalate to 000 for emergency assessment (glucose, bloods, imaging) while giving immediate airway and seizure first aid.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Delaying 000 to gather more history, or reassuring the carer that it is likely just his baseline epilepsy/disability.
